subsystem: libyang XML parser: lyxml_parse_elem(), the recursive-descent routine that parses a single XML element (its name, optional namespace prefix, attributes, text/CDATA/mixed content, comments, PIs, and nested child elements) out of an in-memory XML document buffer. libyang is a YANG data-modeling library; this parser feeds NETCONF/RESTCONF/sysrepo/netopeer2 configuration and RPC processing.
provenance: Identified by the libyang naming conventions and type names: the ly_ctx context handle, struct lyxml_elem / struct lyxml_attr / struct lyxml_ns node types, the lydict_insert / lydict_insert_zc string-dictionary API, the LY_* / LYE_* / LY_VLOG_* / LYXML_* macro families, LOGVAL/LOGMEM diagnostics, and helper names lyxml_getutf8, is_xmlnamestartchar, is_xmlnamechar, parse_attr, parse_text, parse_ignore, lyxml_add_child, lyxml_get_ns. These are the public/internal identifiers of CESNET libyang (src/xml.c). The header banner sample tag 'primevul_c_validation_0224_fixed' and the review filename cwe674 both point at an uncontrolled-recursion class; the code is the FIXED variant, which adds the LY_RECURSION_LIMIT depth guard.
trust_boundary: The const char *data buffer is the untrusted input: it is the raw XML document text. In libyang's deployment (NETCONF/RESTCONF servers such as netopeer2, sysrepo clients) this XML arrives over the network from remote, possibly unauthenticated peers, or from attacker-supplied configuration/model files. The attacker fully controls the byte content, the element nesting depth, element/prefix names, attribute names/values, and content runs. There is NO explicit end pointer or length passed for data — bounding is purely by the NUL terminator and by the pointer arithmetic the parser performs, so every walk (while (*c), lyxml_getutf8, the strncmp probes) trusts that the buffer is correctly NUL-terminated. The recursion depth (bt_count) is an internal counter but is driven entirely by attacker-chosen nesting.

what_the_code_does.explanation: The function signature takes the parse context ctx, the input pointer data, an out-parameter len (unsigned int*) that will report how many bytes of data this call consumed, the parent element to attach to (NULL at the document root), an options bitmask (e.g. LYXML_PARSE_NOMIXEDCONTENT), and bt_count, a recursion-depth counter incremented on each nested call. The locals set up a cursor c walking data, scratch pointers start/e/lws, uc for the decoded UTF-8 code point, str for temporary allocations, and prefix/prefix_len for an optional namespace prefix (e.g. the 'ns' in <ns:foo>). elem is the element being built; size is the reusable byte-count written by the various helper parsers. nons_flag and closed_flag are state flags: whether an explicit no-namespace (xmlns="") was seen and whether a closing tag was matched.

The consumed-length out-parameter is zeroed up front so every early-return path reports zero progress. The recursion guard is the security-relevant addition in this 'fixed' sample: if bt_count exceeds LY_RECURSION_LIMIT the parse is rejected before descending further, capping stack depth for deeply nested XML. The next check requires the element to begin with '<'; if not, the routine bails immediately. Note dereference of *c assumes data points at a readable, NUL-terminated buffer.

After the '<', c is advanced past it and e is set to the start of the element name. lyxml_getutf8 decodes one UTF-8 code point at e and writes the number of bytes it consumed into size. The decoded code point uc must be a valid XML NameStartChar (letter/underscore/colon per the XML grammar); otherwise the element is rejected. On success e is advanced by size bytes to the next character. Crucially lyxml_getutf8 receives only a pointer e — no remaining-length bound — so it must self-limit on the NUL terminator.

This loop scans the remaining NameChar bytes of the element name, decoding one code point per iteration and advancing e by size. When a ':' is encountered the text before it is a namespace prefix: prefix_len is computed as the pointer difference e - c (bytes from name start to the colon), a check rejects a second colon (multiple colons) and LY_CHECK_ERR_GOTO(prefix, ...) rejects a second prefix, then a prefix_len+1 buffer is allocated, the prefix bytes are memcpy'd in and NUL-terminated, and c is moved past the colon so the local name follows. The allocation and copy are both bounded by the same pointer-difference prefix_len, so this copy itself is self-consistent. The loop terminates when getutf8 yields a non-NameChar (including the NUL).

If the name scan ran into the NUL terminator (!*e), the document ended mid-tag: an EOF diagnostic is logged, the prefix buffer freed, and NULL returned. Otherwise the element node is zero-allocated with calloc; the LY_CHECK_ERR_RETURN macro handles allocation failure by freeing prefix, logging out-of-memory, and returning NULL. This is a correct NULL-check on the allocation result.

The new element's sibling links are initialised (prev points to itself, the libyang circular-prev convention), and if a parent was supplied the element is attached as a child. The element name — the bytes from c (after any prefix colon) to e — is interned into libyang's string dictionary with length e - c. c is then advanced to e, positioning the cursor just past the element name for tag-body processing.

The process: label is the re-entry point after each attribute is parsed. ign_xmlws skips XML whitespace. If the next two bytes are '/>' the tag is an empty element: c advances past them, an empty content string is interned, and closed_flag is set. If instead the byte is '>', the opening tag ended and element content follows: c advances past '>' and lws (leading-whitespace tracker for mixed-content detection) is reset. The strncmp("/>", c, 2) reads two bytes at c, again trusting NUL-termination bounds.

This is the main content loop, running while *c is non-NUL. The first branch handles the end tag '</': it re-scans a name the same way the opening tag did, validates the namespace prefix matches (memcmp of prefix against the closing tag's prefix bytes), then materialises the closing name into a size+1 malloc'd buffer str (bounded by size = e - c), NUL-terminates it, and compares it against elem->name by both length and memcmp. On mismatch it errors; on match it frees str, requires the tag to close with '>', interns empty content if there was none, sets closed_flag and breaks out of the content loop. The malloc(size+1) / memcpy(e-c) pair is internally consistent (same length), and the allocation is NULL-checked.

Processing instructions ('<?' ... '?>') and comments ('<!--' ... '-->') are skipped via parse_ignore, which scans for the terminating delimiter and writes the consumed byte count into size; c is advanced by that amount. If parse_ignore fails (unterminated), the parse errors. The lws formatting-whitespace tracker is cleared since whitespace before a PI/comment is not content. The correctness of c += size depends entirely on parse_ignore returning an accurate, in-bounds length.

A '<![CDATA[' sequence routes to the store_content text handler. A bare '<' that is not an end tag/PI/comment/CDATA is a nested child element: if the parent already has text content this is mixed content — rejected under LYXML_PARSE_NOMIXEDCONTENT, otherwise the existing content is re-homed into a synthetic child node and the MIXED flag set. Then the parser RECURSES via lyxml_parse_elem(ctx, c, &size, elem, options, bt_count + 1) to parse the child, incrementing the depth counter. On success c is advanced by the child's reported consumed length size. This recursive call is the core of the CWE-674 surface: nesting depth is attacker-controlled and each level consumes a stack frame.

Whitespace is remembered in lws (it may turn out to be significant content) and skipped. The store_content branch (also jumped to from CDATA/leading-whitespace cases) reads a text run up to the next '<' via parse_text, which returns a heap string and writes the consumed length into size. It errors only if BOTH str is NULL and size is 0. The text is interned zero-copy (lydict_insert_zc takes ownership of str) and c advances by size. If the element already had child elements this text is mixed content, handled the same way as before. The advance c += size relies on parse_text producing an accurate, forward-progressing length.

This is the else branch of the '/>' vs '>' decision at line 79: when the tag body still has attributes, parse_attr consumes one attribute, writing its byte length into size and returning the attr node (NULL on error). c advances by size. If the attribute is a namespace declaration (LYXML_ATTR_NS), the code decides whether it is the default namespace, an explicit no-namespace (xmlns=""), or a prefixed namespace matching this element's prefix (strncmp bounded by prefix_len + 1, which includes the NUL). It then loops back to process: to consume the next attribute or the tag terminator. This goto-based loop is an iterative re-parse, not recursion, and advances by each attribute's length.

On success the consumed length is reported to the caller as *len = c - data (the pointer distance the cursor advanced), which the parent uses in its own c += size. If no closing tag was matched, closed_flag is unset and the element is rejected. Otherwise unresolved attribute prefixes are resolved against the namespace scope, an inherited namespace is looked up from the parent when the element declared none, the prefix buffer is freed, and the element is returned. The error label frees the (possibly partial) element tree via lyxml_free and frees prefix. prefix is freed exactly once on every path (each early return frees it, and success/error each free it once), so there is no double-free here.

what_could_go_wrong.code: child = lyxml_parse_elem(ctx, c, &size, elem, options, bt_count + 1);
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Uncontrolled recursion / stack exhaustion (CWE-674). Every nested XML element triggers a recursive self-call with bt_count + 1, so a document like ... (or any deeply nested tree) drives the C call stack one frame deeper per level. Each frame is sizable (a dozen locals plus the callee's own state), so without a cap an attacker crashes the process (DoS) by sending sufficiently nested XML over NETCONF/RESTCONF. The guard at lines 18-21 (if (bt_count > LY_RECURSION_LIMIT)) is exactly the mitigation, and its presence is direct evidence the authors hit this. The remaining invariant the implementation MUST uphold is that LY_RECURSION_LIMIT is chosen conservatively relative to the real per-frame stack cost and the smallest supported thread-stack size — if the constant is set too high (or the deployment uses small stacks, e.g. worker threads), LY_RECURSION_LIMIT frames still overflow the stack before the check ever rejects the input.

Off-by-one / boundary of the recursion cap (CWE-674, boundary variant). The comparison uses strict '>' , so a call with bt_count == LY_RECURSION_LIMIT is still allowed to proceed and recurse once more to depth LY_RECURSION_LIMIT + 1 before the next frame trips the guard. This is only a one-frame slack and is benign if LY_RECURSION_LIMIT already has margin, but it must be confirmed that the limit's headroom accounts for the +1 and for any additional helper-call frames (parse_attr/parse_text/lyxml_getutf8) that deepen the stack within each level.

Out-of-bounds read at the buffer tail (CWE-125), latent in lyxml_getutf8 (not shown). getutf8 is handed only a pointer e with no remaining-length argument; it decodes a multi-byte UTF-8 sequence and reports the byte count in size. If the buffer ends with a UTF-8 lead byte that advertises a 2-4 byte sequence but the continuation bytes fall on or past the NUL terminator, a naive decoder reads past the end of data. All the name-scanning loops (lines 31, 37, 57, 102, 108, 123) and the subsequent e += size advances trust this decode. The implementation must guarantee getutf8 stops at the NUL and never consults bytes beyond it; this is the same class as the documented libyang lyxml_getutf8 over-read. Confirm getutf8 validates continuation-byte presence before dereferencing them.

Potential non-terminating loop / no forward progress (CWE-835), latent in the helpers. The content loop while (*c) advances the cursor only by the helper-reported size. If parse_text (or parse_attr at line 246, or parse_ignore at 165) can return with size == 0 while *c stays non-NUL and no error is raised — here parse_text errors only when BOTH str is NULL and size is 0, so a non-NULL str with size 0 slips through — then c += size does not advance and the while (*c) loop spins forever, a CPU-exhaustion DoS on attacker-crafted input. The invariant the implementation must uphold: every helper that writes *size and returns success MUST guarantee size > 0 (strict forward progress) for any non-empty remaining input. Confirm parse_text/parse_attr/parse_ignore can never return success with a zero consumed length.

Unbounded scanning depends on a NUL-terminated input contract (CWE-20 / CWE-125). lyxml_parse_elem receives no length/end pointer for data; every probe — this *c dereference, the strncmp(c, ...) tag tests, ign_xmlws, and the while (*c) content loop — assumes data is a valid, NUL-terminated C string. If a caller passes a buffer that is not NUL-terminated (or a slice that does not include the terminator), the parser reads past the end of the allocation, an out-of-bounds read that can crash or leak adjacent memory into content/name strings. The invariant: all callers of lyxml_parse_elem (e.g. lyxml_parse_mem) must NUL-terminate the XML buffer before entry. Confirm the public entry point guarantees termination.

what_could_go_wrong.code: c += size;      /* move after processed child element */
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Cursor advance trusts the child's reported length (parser-arithmetic correctness). The parent advances its own cursor by the *len value the recursive child wrote (line 272, *len = c - data). If a nested parse could ever report a size larger than the bytes it actually validated, the parent's c would jump past unparsed/invalid bytes or past the buffer end, desynchronising the parse and enabling downstream out-of-bounds dereferences on the next while (*c). The invariant: the returned consumed-length must always equal exactly the number of in-bounds bytes the child consumed, and callers rely on the child having returned NULL (checked here at line 207) whenever it did not reach a valid closing tag. This is low risk given the child's own closed_flag gate but should be confirmed to be monotonic and bounded by the remaining buffer.

summary: This is libyang's recursive XML element parser operating directly on untrusted document bytes. Its dominant risk is CWE-674 uncontrolled recursion via nested elements (line 206), for which this 'fixed' revision adds an LY_RECURSION_LIMIT depth guard (line 18) — safety now hinges entirely on that constant being conservative relative to real stack-frame cost. Secondary latent risks live in unshown helpers: a tail-of-buffer out-of-bounds read in lyxml_getutf8, a possible non-advancing (infinite) content loop if a helper returns a zero consumed length on success, and reliance on the input being NUL-terminated. The explicit copy sites (prefix/str) are internally length-consistent and NULL-checked, so they are not the weak points.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: lyxml_parse_elem(struct ly_ctx*, const char *data, unsigned int *len, struct lyxml_elem *parent, int options, int bt_count) signature (lines 2-3)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Recursive parser over untrusted data with a depth counter — the central CWE-674 surface.
Attacker-driven recursion depth counter; safety depends on the limit check that consumes it.
The depth cap constant; its numeric value versus per-frame stack cost is the safety hinge.
Strict '>' allows one extra level (LY_RECURSION_LIMIT+1) — boundary to confirm.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: const char *data parameter with no length/end argument (line 2)
reviewed_anchors.reason: All scanning is NUL-bounded only; unterminated input causes OOB reads (contract).

reviewed_anchors.anchor: unsigned int *len out-parameter (line 2), set at line 272 *len = c - data
reviewed_anchors.reason: Parent advances by this value (line 210); must be exact and bounded.
Length-less UTF-8 decode near buffer tail is a latent OOB-read (CWE-125).
Success with size==0 would prevent forward progress — potential infinite loop (CWE-835).
goto process loop advances only by size; a zero-length success would loop forever.
Dereferences rely on NUL termination of data (part of the input-contract finding).

reviewed_anchors.anchor: prefix = malloc((prefix_len + 1) * sizeof *prefix) (line 50) with memcpy(prefix, c, prefix_len) (line 52)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Allocation size and copy length are the same pointer-difference prefix_len (+1 for NUL); NULL-checked at line 51 — internally consistent, no overflow.
Bounded pointer difference within the scanned name; not attacker-oversizable beyond the buffer.
Guards against a leaked/overwritten prefix allocation; a defensive check, not a vulnerability.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: str = malloc((size + 1) * sizeof *str) (line 132) with memcpy(str, c, e - c) (line 134), size = e - c (line 131)
reviewed_anchors.reason: size equals e - c, so allocation and copy lengths match; NULL-checked at line 133 — internally consistent.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: elem = calloc(1, sizeof *elem) (line 66) / child = calloc(1, sizeof *child) (lines 199, 235)
reviewed_anchors.reason: All three allocations are NULL-checked via LY_CHECK_ERR_RETURN/LY_CHECK_ERR_GOTO — no unchecked-malloc deref.
Interns exactly the scanned name span; length is a bounded pointer difference.
strncmp is bounded by prefix_len+1 and stops at the NUL of the shorter string — no over-read from the compare itself.
Same size==0 forward-progress concern as parse_text, already captured by the CWE-835 finding; not separately distinct.
Iterative attribute consumption, bounded by input length; not recursion and not separately overflowing (progress concern folded into the parse_attr finding).
prefix is freed exactly once on every return path and elem freed once on error — no double-free/leak in the shown code.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: unsigned int size / int uc / const char *lws locals (lines 7, 13, 6)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Scratch locals reused per helper call; carry no cross-call security contract of their own.
Pure character-class predicates on an already-decoded code point; no memory access of their own.

findings.title: Uncontrolled recursion / stack exhaustion on deeply nested XML
findings.anchor: child = lyxml_parse_elem(ctx, c, &size, elem, options, bt_count + 1);
findings.code: child = lyxml_parse_elem(ctx, c, &size, elem, options, bt_count + 1);
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0015_cwe674.c (recursive call, line 206) and implementation (file not provided) for the LY_RECURSION_LIMIT definition
findings.invariant: Nesting depth must be capped BEFORE recursing, and LY_RECURSION_LIMIT must be small enough that LY_RECURSION_LIMIT (plus helper) stack frames cannot exceed the smallest supported thread stack.
findings.failure_mode: An attacker sends XML nested many levels deep (<a><a>...); each level recurses one frame deeper. Without a conservative cap the C stack overflows and the process crashes — a remote DoS on NETCONF/RESTCONF servers. This 'fixed' revision adds the guard at lines 18-21; the residual risk is that LY_RECURSION_LIMIT is set too high for the real per-frame cost or for small worker-thread stacks.
findings.what_to_confirm: The numeric value of LY_RECURSION_LIMIT and the measured worst-case stack frame size of lyxml_parse_elem plus its callees, against the minimum stack size in all deployments.

findings.title: Out-of-bounds read decoding a UTF-8 sequence at the buffer tail
findings.where_it_lives: implementation (file not provided) — lyxml_getutf8; call sites at lines 31, 37, 57, 102, 108, 123 in corpus/train/train_0015_cwe674.c
findings.invariant: lyxml_getutf8 must detect the NUL terminator / end of buffer and never read continuation bytes that lie on or past it; the number written to size must not advance the cursor beyond the terminator.
findings.failure_mode: If the document ends with a UTF-8 lead byte claiming a 2-4 byte sequence but the continuation bytes fall on/after the NUL, a decoder that reads them dereferences memory past the end of data — an out-of-bounds read that can crash or leak adjacent heap bytes into parsed names/content. All name scans trust this decode with no remaining-length bound.
findings.what_to_confirm: That lyxml_getutf8 checks for the terminating NUL before reading each continuation byte and returns a size that never steps past it.

findings.title: Possible non-terminating content loop when a helper reports zero consumed length
findings.anchor: str = parse_text(ctx, c, '<', &size); if (!str && !size) { goto error; } ... c += size;
findings.bug_class: Loop with no forward progress (CPU-exhaustion DoS)
findings.where_it_lives: implementation (file not provided) — parse_text/parse_attr/parse_ignore; the driving loop is while (*c) at line 91 in corpus/train/train_0015_cwe674.c
findings.invariant: Every helper that returns success while *c is non-NUL MUST write a strictly positive size, so the outer while (*c) loop always advances the cursor.
findings.failure_mode: The error gate here fires only when BOTH str is NULL and size is 0. If parse_text can succeed (non-NULL str) with size == 0 on some crafted input, c += size makes no progress and the while (*c) loop at line 91 spins forever, pinning a CPU — a remote DoS. The same concern applies to parse_attr (line 246) and parse_ignore (line 165) driven by the goto process loop.
findings.what_to_confirm: That parse_text, parse_attr, and parse_ignore can never return a success/non-error result with a zero consumed length for non-empty input.

findings.title: Parser relies on an implicit NUL-terminated input contract (no length bound)
findings.anchor: if (*c != '<') { return NULL; }  /* and while (*c) at line 91, strncmp probes */
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read via missing input-length validation
findings.where_it_lives: implementation (file not provided) — callers of lyxml_parse_elem such as lyxml_parse_mem; dereferences throughout corpus/train/train_0015_cwe674.c (lines 23, 81, 91, 92, 158, 169, 180)
findings.invariant: The data buffer passed to lyxml_parse_elem must be NUL-terminated so that every *c, strncmp(c,...), ign_xmlws, and getutf8 scan is bounded by the terminator.
findings.failure_mode: lyxml_parse_elem takes no length/end argument, so if any caller passes a non-terminated buffer or a slice excluding the terminator, all scanning walks off the end of the allocation — an out-of-bounds read that can crash or leak adjacent memory into names/content. The whole parser's memory safety is delegated to this unstated contract.
findings.what_to_confirm: That the public entry point always NUL-terminates (or copies-and-terminates) the XML buffer before calling lyxml_parse_elem.

findings.title: Recursion cap uses strict greater-than (allows one extra level)
findings.bug_class: Off-by-one boundary on the recursion cap
findings.invariant: The effective maximum recursion depth (LY_RECURSION_LIMIT + 1, given the strict '>') plus the deepest helper-call chain must fit within the available stack with margin.
findings.failure_mode: Because the test is strict '>', a call already at bt_count == LY_RECURSION_LIMIT proceeds and recurses to depth LY_RECURSION_LIMIT + 1 before the next frame is rejected. This is only one extra frame and is harmless if the limit has headroom, but if LY_RECURSION_LIMIT was tuned to be exactly the maximum tolerable depth the +1 could be the frame that overflows.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether LY_RECURSION_LIMIT accounts for the inclusive extra level and for helper frames, or whether it should be a '>=' comparison.

audit_checklist: Confirm the numeric value of LY_RECURSION_LIMIT and measure worst-case stack usage per lyxml_parse_elem frame (including parse_attr/parse_text/getutf8) against the smallest supported thread stack.
Verify lyxml_getutf8 bounds every multi-byte decode on the NUL terminator and never reads continuation bytes past the end of data.
Prove parse_text, parse_attr, and parse_ignore always report a strictly positive consumed length on success, so the while (*c) and goto process loops cannot spin without progress.
Confirm every public caller of lyxml_parse_elem passes a NUL-terminated data buffer (no length argument is available to the parser).
Check that the recursive child's returned *len (line 272) is always exact and bounded by the remaining buffer before the parent does c += size (line 210).
Confirm prefix is freed exactly once on all paths and elem is not double-freed between the error label and lyxml_free.
Decide whether the recursion guard should be '>=' rather than '>' to remove the extra allowed level.
